coredata.dc:title: Dual emissive Cl, N-codoped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of amphotericin B in milk and wastewater
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Bao, Guang-Ming
Cui, Shun-Qiang
Xia, Yi-Fan
Dou, Zhen-Chong
Wei, Xia
Cai, Zhi-Qiang
Chen, Peiyao
Zhang, Yi
Chen, Yu-Xin
Yuan, Hou-Qun
coredata.dc:description: Amphotericin B (AmB) is an antifungal drug used in dairy farming. It is significant to monitor AmB in milk and environmental water as AmB is severely toxic. Herein, dual emissive Cl, N-codoped carbon dots (Cl, N-CDs) were facilely synthesized and explored as fluorescent probes for specific recognition of AmB among sixty-eight common substances. The Cl, N-CDs possess long emission wavelengths (650 and 482 nm), large stokes shift (240 nm), and high quantum yield (22.06 %). The fluorescence of Cl, N-CDs can be specifically quenched by AmB with a linear concentration range of 0.1–6 μM, a detection limit of 6.67 nM, and a response time within 1 min. Moreover, the Cl, N-CDs can be applied for the determination of AmB in milk and wastewater with good recoveries (92.00–110.00 %). This work provides a facile method for the preparation of Cl, N-codoped CDs with excellent fluorescence properties, and develops a new approach for fluorescence quantification of AmB in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent detection
Amphotericin B
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of Cl, N-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and HRTEM (inset) images of Cl, N-CDs; (b) the size distribution of Cl, N-CDs; (c) AFM image of Cl, N-CDs; (d) Raman spectrum of Cl, N-CDs; (e) FT-IR spectrum of Cl, N-CDs; High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (f); N 1s (g); O 1s (h); and Cl 2p (i).

(a) The fluorescence spectra of Cl, N-CDs after immersion in DMF for several days; (b) Fluorescence intensities at 482 and 650 nm of Cl, N-CDs in DMF containing 10 mM NaCl for several days; (c) Fluorescence intensities at 482 and 650 nm of Cl, N-CDs with different temperature; (d) Fluorescence intensities at 482 and 650 nm of Cl, N-CDs after irradiation under UV lamp for 90 min.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of Cl, N-CDs with different concentrations of AmB (0–30 μM); (b) The I 0/I values at 482 nm and 650 nm vary with the concentration of AmB in the range of 0.1–30 μM; (c) The linearity of I 0/I values at 482 nm and 650 nm with the concentrations of AmB in the range of 0.1–6 μM. Error bars are ± SD, n = 3. Fluorescence response of Cl, N-CDs toward various veterinary drugs, various natural amino acids, some small organic molecules and ions (d), (f), (h) at 482 nm; (e), (g), (i) at 650 nm.

Suppressed efficiency (E) of observed and corrected at 482 nm (a) and 650 nm (b) after adding different concentrations of AmB to Cl, N-CDs; The Stern-Volmer plots for the fluorescence of Cl, N-CDs quenched by different concentrations of AmB at 482 nm (c) and 650 nm (d) after IFE correction. F cor,0 and F cor were the corrected fluorescence intensity of Cl, N-CDs without/with AmB, respectively. The data were taken from Table 3.
The synthesis of Cl, N-CDs and its fluorescence sensing toward AmB.
The comparison of fluorescence properties of Cl-doped CDs.
Results for the quantification of AmB in milk and wastewater samples. Note: all concentrations are expressed as the average values of three measurements.
IFE of AmB on the Fluorescence of Cl, N-CDs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: aA ex and bA em are the absorbance of Cl, N-CDs upon the addition of AmB at 410 and 482/650 nm, respectively. cCF (Correction factor), calculated as F cor/F obsd. The maximum value of the CF could not exceed 3; otherwise, the correction was not convincing. dF obsd is the measured FL of Cl, N-CDs upon the addition of AmB at 482 or 650 nm. eFcor is the FL corrected with (eq.2) by removing IFE from Fobsd. fE obsd = 1 − F obsd/F obsd,0. F obsd,0 and F obsd are the observed fluorescence intensities of Cl, N-CDs in the absence/presence of AmB, respectively. gE cor = 1 − F cor/Fcor,0. F cor,0 and F cor are the corrected fluorescence intensities of Cl, N-CDs in the absence/presence of AmB, respectively.

head.ce:title: Dual emissive Cl, N-codoped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of amphotericin B in milk and wastewater

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National “111” Center for Cellular Regulation and Molecular Pharmaceutics, Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering (Ministry of Education), Hubei Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbiology, School of Life and Health Sciences, Hubei University of Technology, Wuhan 430068, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National “111” Center for Cellular Regulation and Molecular Pharmaceutics
Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering (Ministry of Education)
Hubei Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbiology
School of Life and Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National “111” Center for Cellular Regulation and Molecular Pharmaceutics, Key Laboratory of Fermentation Engineering (Ministry of Education), Hubei Key Laboratory of Industrial Microbiology, School of Life and Health Sciences, Hubei University of Technology, Wuhan 430068, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amphotericin B (AmB) is an antifungal drug used in dairy farming. It is significant to monitor AmB in milk and environmental water as AmB is severely toxic. Herein, dual emissive Cl, N-codoped carbon dots (Cl, N-CDs) were facilely synthesized and explored as fluorescent probes for specific recognition of AmB among sixty-eight common substances. The Cl, N-CDs possess long emission wavelengths (650 and 482 nm), large stokes shift (240 nm), and high quantum yield (22.06 %). The fluorescence of Cl, N-CDs can be specifically quenched by AmB with a linear concentration range of 0.1–6 μM, a detection limit of 6.67 nM, and a response time within 1 min. Moreover, the Cl, N-CDs can be applied for the determination of AmB in milk and wastewater with good recoveries (92.00–110.00 %). This work provides a facile method for the preparation of Cl, N-codoped CDs with excellent fluorescence properties, and develops a new approach for fluorescence quantification of AmB in real samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Amphotericin B (AmB) is a polyene drug produced by the fermentation of Streptomyces, which has broad-spectrum antifungal activity against Candida, Bacillus dermatitis, coccidioides, etc [1]. At present, AmB is a commonly used antifungal drug to prevent and treat fungal diseases in dairy farming. Nevertheless, the overuse of AmB will result in large residues in milk. It is found that AmB is severely toxic which can lead to kidney damage, liver damage, thrombophlebitis, rash, peripheral neuritis, and so on [2]. Meanwhile, the discharge of wastewater generated during the fermentation of AmB by streptomyces would lead to environmental pollution. Thus, it is significant to monitor AmB in milk and environment water.

Currently, there are very few references about the detection of AmB based on chromatography, which are mainly in biological samples [3]. Although chromatography has the benefits of good detection performance with high sensitivity, it has the disadvantages of high cost, time-consuming, and complex operation. Hence, it is required to explore a new analysis method with high sensitivity, good selectivity, simplicity, time-saving, and low cost for the detection of AmB. In the past decades, the fluorescence detection method has received tremendous attention due to its merits of superior sensitivity, rapid response, visualization, and simple operation [4–6]. Many fluorescent materials have been explored for sensing various ions, and small organic molecules, while no fluorescence analysis was used for the determination of AmB.

Carbon dots (CDs), newly emerging excellent fluorescent nanomaterials, have received widespread attention owing to their unique optical properties, excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, facile synthesis, and abundant raw materials [7,8]. Owing to these distinct merits, CDs have been used in various areas including bioimaging [9], photocatalysis [10], drug delivery [11], optoelectronics [12], and fluorescent bio/chemo sensors [13–15]. Many natural precursors such as food products, leaves, algae, and etc are applied for the synthesis of CDs, while most of these CDs show short wavelengths of blue emission and relatively low photoluminescence quantum yield (QY), which greatly hampers their applications and developments in bioimaging, sensing, and optoelectronics [16]. On the other hand, organic precursors are high purity and quantum yield [17]. Recent studies have shown that element doping and surface modification are the two effective approaches to tuning the bandgap and surface functional groups of CDs, which enables to improvement of the luminescence properties. Currently, many heteroatom doping CDs have been explored for efficient detection of various substances. For example, Hu et al. reported Se, N, Cl-doped CDs with dual emissive bands centered at 625 and 679 nm and a high QY of 23.6 % for the detection of malachite green [18]. Yang et al. demonstrated N, Cl-CDs with emission at 570 nm and QY of 15 % for determination of organophosphate pesticides and acetylcholinesterase activity [19]. Wang et al. reported a P, Cl-CDs with emission at 590 nm and QY of 15 % for the detection of Fe3+ ions [20].

Citric acid, cyanuric chloride, DMF, Clindamycin phosphate (CMP), Ractopamine hydrochloride (RAC), and Butoconazole nitrate (BN) were obtained from Aladdin Reagents (China). Urea, amphotericin B, Albendazole (ABZ), Fenbendazole (FBZ), Flucytosine (5-FC), Griseofulvin (GRF), Diclazuril (DIC), Natamycin (NAT), Estradiol (E2), Florfenicol (FF), Erythromycin (ERY), Sulfamethazine (SMT), Sulfadiazine (SDZ), Sulfathiazole (STZ), Diethylstilbestrol (DES), Thiamphenicol (TAP), Tylosin phosphate (TYL), Streptomycin sulfate (SS), Neomycin sulfate (NMS), Chloramphenicol (CAP), Tilmicosin (TMS), Clindamycin hydrochloride (CLH), Doxycycline (DOX), Nystatin (NYS), Butenafine hydrochloride (BTF) which were obtained from Macklin Reagent (China). L-Cysteine (Cys), L-Phenylalanine (Phe), L-Glycine (Gly), L-Alanine (Ala), L-Glutamine (Gln), L-Lysine (Lys), L-Arginine (Arg), L-Glutamate (Glu), L-Leucine (Leu), L-Proline (Pro), L-Tryptophan (Trp), L-Serine (Ser), L-Threonine (Thr), L-Isoleucine (Ile), L-Histidine (His), L-Methionine (Met), L-Asparticacid (Asp), Tyrosine (Tyr), L-Asparagine (Asn), Valine (Val), Nicotinicacid (NA), Folicacid (FA), VitaminB5 (VB5), VitaminC (VC), VitaminH (VH), Cholesterol (CHOL), Glucose (Glu), Galactose (Gal), Lactose (Lac) were obtained from Lanji Biology (Shanghai). I− and K+ which were obtained from Xilong Chemical Reagent (China). Cl−, SO4 2−, HCO3 −, NO3 −, Ca2+, Mg2+, Na+, Fe2+, Fe3+ and Zn2+ were obtained from Fuchen Chemical Reagent (Tianjin).

Field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on JEM-2100. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out on a Bruker-D8 Advanced using CuKα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) results were obtained from THS-103. Fourier Transform infrared spectra results were recorded by Thermofisher Nicolet iS10 using KBr particles in the range of 4000–400 cm−1. Fluorometric spectra were recorded on a HORIBA FluoroMax-4 using xenon lamps. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectra (UV–vis) data were collected on an INESA N4S spectrophotometer.

Cl, N-CDs were prepared by a solvothermal method. The mixture of citric acid (0.961 g, 5 mmol), urea (1.802 g, 30 mmol), cyanuric chloride (0.461 g, 2.5 mmol), and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, 10 mL) was ultrasound for 10 min, then transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (25 mL) and heated to 180 °C for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, a deep brownish-red solution was obtained, which was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particle precipitation. Then, 20 mL of anhydrous ethanol was added to the supernatant, and the resulting precipitates were collected by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The collected precipitates were then washed with 15 mL ethanol three times. Finally, the residues were dried under vacuum for 8 h to give black powders (Cl, N-CDs). Yield: 177.9 mg.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence quantum yield (QY) of Cl, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The fluorescence QY of Cl, N-CDs was measured following the reported methods by using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference (QY = 54 %) [37]. The corresponding QY of Cl, N-CDs was calculated by
(1)
Q c
=
Q r
(
I c
A r
η
c
2
/
I r
A c
η
r
2
)
× 100 %
Where Q, I, and A represent the QY, integrated emission intensity, and absorption intensity at 360 nm, respectively. η is the refractive index of the solvent (η DMF = 1.43). The subscripts “r” and “c” present quinine sulfate and Cl, N-CDs, respectively.
3.0 mg of Cl, N-CDs was dissolved in 40 mL DMF and stored at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra were measured daily for 7 consecutive days.

To the Cl, N-CDs DMF solution (0.075 mg/mL, 2 mL), 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, and 30 μL of standard high concentration of AmB (2 mM, DMF) was added, respectively, and the final concentrations of AmB were 0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 μM in sequence. The fluorescence spectrum of each mixture was then recorded.
The fluorescence response of Cl, N-CDs toward various veterinary drugs, amino acids, some vitamins, carbonhydrates, and some common ions were studied.

Each of these interferences (12 μL, 10 mM) was added into Cl, N-CDs DMF solution (2 mL, 0.075 mg/mL), respectively, and mixed well. The final concentration of each interference was 60 μM, and the fluorescence spectrum of each mixture was then recorded.

For the anti-interference study, AmB (30 μL, 2 mM) was added to the mixture of Cl, N-CDs containing the above interferences. The final concentration of AmB and each interference was 30 and 60 μM, respectively. The fluorescence spectrum of each mixture was then measured.
The fluorescence emission spectra of Cl, N-CDs (2 mL, 0.075 mg/mL) with AmB (30 μ M) were measured until the fluorescence intensity reached a plateau.

The milk sample was bought from a local supermarket (Wuhan, China). Typically, 5 mL acetonitrile was added into 2 mL milk, the mixture was shaken for 3 min and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was collected and filtered through a 0.22 μ m organic filter membrane to obtain a milk pretreatment solution.

The wastewater was obtained from the Jiangxia District of Wuhan City, China. First, the wastewater was filtered to remove insoluble substances. Then, 0.3 g of Cleanert ODS C18 and Cleanert PSA were added into 10 mL filtrate and vortexed for 3 min, followed by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min. The supernatant was collected and filtered with a 0.22 μm water phase filter membrane to obtain the wastewater pretreatment solution.

20 μ L of the pretreatment solution was added into 2 mL Cl, N-CDs (0.075 mg/mL) DMF solution, and 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0, and 6.0 μL of 2 mM AmB (in DMF) was added into the mixture, respectively. Then the fluorescence spectrum of each mixture was recorded. For the linear relationship, the final AmB concentrations were 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0, and 6.0 μM (Fig. S9). For the recovery test, the final AmB concentrations were 0.7, 1.5, 2.5, and 3.5 μM (Table 2 ).

First, the high-concentration solutions of AmB were prepared in DMF solvent (0.2 and 2.0 mM). Then, 0.5, 1.0, and 2.5 μL of 0.2 mM AmB, and 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, and 3.0 μL of 2.0 mM AmB were added into 980 μL of the pretreatment solution, respectively. Additional DMF solvent was added to adjust the total volume of each sample to 1.0 mL, and the final concentrations of AmB were 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0, and 6.0 μM for the standard linear relationship (Fig. S10). For the recovery test, the final concentrations of AmB were 0.7, 1.5, 2.5, and 3.5 μM (Table 2).

The HPLC analysis was performed according to the previous work with small modifications [38]. Injection volume: 10 μL; flow rate: 0.8 mL·min−1; Column oven temperature: 45 °C; mobile phase: phase A: CH3CN, phase B: NH4Ac-HAc buffer (pH = 5.8) with CH3CN (90:10, v/v), and phase A: phase B was 4:6 (v/v).
All the above measurements for each concentration were performed three times. All the fluorescence sensing spectra were recorded under the excitation wavelength of 410 nm with slits of 1.5/1.5 nm.

TEM images of Cl, N-CDs are given in Fig. 1 a, Cl, N-CDs are well dispersed quasi-spherical, and uniform in size. The lattice spacing of 0.27 nm (Fig. 1a inset) was observed by high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) which corresponds to the lattice fringes of the (100) plane in graphite [39]. The diameters of Cl, N-CDs range from 2.3 to 5.0 nm, with an average diameter of 3.5 nm (Fig. 1b). The height of Cl, N-CDs was estimated to be 1.1–2.6 nm based on atomic force microscopy (AFM) measurements, suggesting the 4–9 layers of graphene-like sheets in Cl, N-CDs (Fig. 1c). The Raman spectrum of Cl, N-CDs presents the disordered D band at 1357 cm−1 and crystalline G band at 1631 cm−1 (Fig. 1d). The ratio of I G/I D was 1.01, implying the high degree of crystallinity in Cl, N-CDs [40,41]. As shown in Fig. S1a, the PXRD results of Cl, N-CDs showed a wide diffraction peak centered at 27°, which corresponds to the (002) graphitic carbon diffraction curve [42]. The surface functional groups of Cl, N-CDs were analyzed by XPS (Fig. S1b) and FT-IR (Fig. 1e) spectra. XPS analysis was used to obtain further insights into the chemical composition, surface functional groups, and nature of bonds of Cl, N-CDs (Table S2). The high-resolution of C 1s demonstrated three peaks at 284.3, 285.6, and 287.6 eV, which are ascribed to C–C/C=C, C–N/C–O, and C=N groups (Fig. 1f) [43–45]. The N 1S spectrum can be fitted with three peaks at 397.9, 399.8, and 401.7 eV, contributing to C=N–C, N–H, and C–NH2 groups (Fig. 1g) [45,46]. The O 1s spectrum is divided into two peaks at 532.73 and 531.28 eV, corresponding to C–O and C=O, respectively (Fig. 1h) [45]. The peaks of Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 are at 198.04 and 199.74 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of Cl 2p (Fig. 1i) [27]. According to FT-IR results (Fig. 1e), the peaks at 3189, 2807, 1348, and 1188 cm−1 are ascribed to the vibrations of O–H/N–H, –CH2, C–N, and C–O, respectively [44,47,48]. The strong bands located at 1713 and 1646 cm−1 are related to the vibrations of –COOH, and C=O/C=N, respectively [49–51]. The stretching vibration of C–Cl was found at 702 cm−1, suggesting the successful doping of Cl element in Cl, N-CDs [20]. Therefore, the FT-IR results confirm the existence of –COOH, –OH, –NH2, and C–Cl groups in Cl, N-CDs.

The optical properties of Cl, N-CDs were studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectra in both H2O and DMF (Fig. S2a, b). In H2O (Fig. S2a), the Cl, N-CDs have a wide absorption band with a main peak at 528 nm and shoulders at 242 and 354 nm. The emission spectrum of Cl, N-CDs in H2O showed a weak broad band with a long tail to red region, and the maximum emission was located at 454 nm under 380 nm excitation. By contrast, the absorption of Cl, N-CDs in DMF (Fig. S2a) had a shoulder at 313 nm and three peaks centered at 269, 411, and 587 nm. The absorption peaks at 269, 313, and 411 nm are attributed to the π-π* transitions of C=O, C=N, and surface state of Cl, N-CDs, while the absorption peak at 578 nm is related to the large-sized conjugated sp2 domain [20,51–53]. The emission spectrum (Fig. S2b) of Cl, N-CDs exhibit dual emission bands in the range of 400–580 and 580–720 nm, and the fluorescence intensity is much stronger than in H2O [54]. The fluorescence color of CDs in DMF and H2O (Fig. S2b inset) is consistent with the CIE diagram (Fig. S3). Thus, the fluorescence properties and sensing performance of Cl, N-CDs were investigated in DMF.

As shown in Fig. S2c, the emissions Cl, N-CDs show excitation-dependent properties. When the excitation wavelength changed from 380 nm to 480 nm, the maximum emission wavelength showed a red-shift from 465 to 535 nm, and the fluorescence intensity was first enhanced and then weakened, reaching the maximum under excitation of 430 nm with maximum emission wavelength at 490 nm [55]. By contrast, for the emission band in 580–720 nm centered at 650 nm, only the emission intensity changed with the increase of excitation wavelength. Such excitation-dependent property is a common phenomenon in luminescent CDs, which is associated with the multiple emission centers and various surface states of CDs [56]. The fluorescence lifetime (τ) of the two bands are 7.72 and 4.68 ns, respectively. The relative QY of Cl, N-CDs in DMF is calculated to be 22.06 % based on quinine sulfate as a reference, which is relatively higher than most of the reported Cl-containing CDs (Table 1). The fluorescence intensity at 650 nm of Cl, N-CDs is the strongest under excitation of 410 nm, thus 410 nm was used as the excitation wavelength for subsequent experiments.

The fluorescence stability of Cl, N-CDs was also studied. As shown in Fig. 2 a and S4a both fluorescence intensities at 482 nm and 650 nm of Cl, N-CDs in DMF barely changed within 7 days, indicating its good fluorescence stability in DMF. Moreover, the fluorescence spectrum of Cl, N-CDs is also stable in DMF containing 10 mM NaCl (Fig. 2b and S4b). In addition, to investigate the influence of temperature on the fluorescence intensity, the Cl, N-CDs solution was stored at different temperatures for 10 min, and the spectrum was then recorded. As depicted in Fig. 2c and S4c, the fluorescence intensity shows no obvious changes, indicating that the fluorescence of Cl, N-CDs is stable in the temperature range of −15 – 100 °C. Furthermore, the fluorescence stability of Cl, N-CDs against photobleaching under UV irradiation was also investigated. The fluorescence intensity of Cl, N-CDs decreased about 24 % at 482 nm and 18 % at 650 nm after irradiation under a 340 nm UV lamp (8 W) for 90 min, indicating the Cl, N-CDs experienced photobleaching (Fig. 2d and S4d).

To evaluate the fluorescence sensing ability, the concentration of Cl, N-CDs was optimized. When the concentration of Cl, N-CDs decreased from 0.1 to 0.0075 mg/mL, both fluorescence intensities at 482 and 650 nm first increased and then decreased (Fig. S5a). The fluorescence intensities reached a maximum when the concentration of Cl, N-CDs was 0.025 mg/mL (Fig. S5a). The maximum wavelength of the blue band remained almost unchanged, while the maximum wavelength of the red band had redshift and decrease of fluorescence intensity gradually with the increase concentration of Cl, N-CDs (Fig. S5b). When the concentration of Cl, N-CDs was 0.075 mg/mL, Cl, N-CDs has relatively long emission wavelength and relatively high fluorescence intensities. Considering that fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength are important for sensing performance, 0.075 mg/mL was used for the subsequent studies.

The fluorescence response of Cl, N-CDs towards various substances was investigated. Twenty-seven different veterinary drugs, twenty natural amino acids, and Twenty-one common substances including some carbohydrates, vitamins, and some inorganic salts were added into Cl, N-CDs DMF solution, respectively, and the fluorescence spectrum of each mixture was recorded. As demonstrated in Fig. S6a-c, the natural amino acids, the selected carbohydrates, vitamins, inorganic salts, and the other twenty-six veterinary drugs showed no obvious change in the fluorescence intensity of Cl, N-CDs, while only AmB can effectively quench the fluorescence intensity at both 482 and 650 nm. The above results showed that the fluorescence of Cl, N-CDs is a highly selective response towards AmB.

In order to investigate the sensitivity of Cl, N-CDs towards AmB, the fluorescence spectra of Cl, N-CDs with different standard concentrations of AmB (0–30 μM) were measured. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, the fluorescence intensity of Cl, N-CDs at 482 nm and 650 nm decreased gradually according to the increase in concentration of AmB. When the concentration of AmB was 30 μ M, the fluorescence intensity at 482 and 650 nm were quenched over 90 %. The fluorescence intensity ratios of both (I 0/I)482 and (I 0/I)650 show nonlinear fitting in the range of 0.1–30 μ M (Fig. 3b), while exhibiting a good linear relationship in the range of 0.1–6 μM between the concentrations of AmB with the equation of (I 0/I)482 = 0.1554 C AmB + 1.0342 (R 2 = 0.9954) for the fluorescence at 482 nm, and the equation of (I 0/I)650 = 0.1397 C AmB + 1.0081 (R 2 = 0.9934) for the fluorescence at 650 nm (Fig. 3c).

The LOD for AmB was calculated to be 6.67 nM for 482 nm and 16.40 nM for 650 nm based on a signal-to-noise (S/N) ratio of 3, which are comparable or lower than the reported LOD values by other analysis methods (Table S1). These results indicate that the synthesized Cl, N-CDs are highly sensitive, and enable trace detection of AmB.

Since real samples such as milk and wastewater contain multiple components, the influences of potential materials such as vitamins, amino acids, carbohydrates, veterinary drugs, and some metal ions (Mn+) were studied. 30 μL 2 mM of AmB was added into Cl, N-CDs solutions containing the interferences (60 μM), and the fluorescence spectrum of each mixture was recorded. As shown in Fig. 3d-i and S7a-c, with the addition of AmB, the fluorescence of Cl, N-CDs containing interferences can be effectively quenched at both 482 and 650 nm. These results showed that Cl, N-CDs exhibit excellent anti-interference for sensing AmB, indicating the possibility for practical application in real samples.

Response time is another important issue for sensors, especially for practical applications. Therefore, the response time of Cl, N-CDs towards AmB was investigated. Fig. S8a and Fig. S8b clearly show that the fluorescence intensities of Cl, N-CDs at 482 nm and 650 nm decreased significantly within 1 min after the addition of AmB (30 μ M), indicating the response of Cl, N-CDs towards AmB is quite fast.

To verify the possibility of practical application, the Cl, N-CDs were applied to detect AmB in milk and wastewater by standard-addition-recovery tests. The linearity of the fluorescence intensity and AmB concentrations in milk and wastewater was presented in Fig. S9a-d. Standard concentrations of AmB solutions were added to the milk and wastewater treatment solution, the final concentration of AmB was 0.7, 1.5, 2.5, and 3.5 μM in the mixture, and their fluorescence spectra were measured. As shown in Table 2, the recoveries of AmB at 482 nm and 650 nm were 94.00–107.14 % and 92.00–110.00 % with good RSD values (n = 3). The results illustrated that Cl, N-CDs could be practically applied for quantification of AmB in real samples.

The decrease in fluorescence intensity of CDs is generally related to the static quenching effect (SQE), dynamic quenching effect (DQE), internal filtration effect (IFE), or fluorescence energy transfer (FRET) [57]. Since the UV–vis absorption spectrum of AmB does not overlap with the emission spectrum of Cl, N-CDs (Fig. S11), the FRET effect is not related to the quenching effect. There is considerable overlap between the absorption and excitation spectra of Cl, N-CDs in the range of 360–440 nm (Fig. S11), thus IFE was first considered for fluorescence suppression. IFE was estimated with Eq. (2) according to the literature [58,59].
(2)
F cor
/
F obsd
=
2 . 3 d
A ex
/
1 - 1
0
- d A ex
1
0
g A em
2 . 3 s
A em
/
1 - 1
0
- s A em
Where s, g, and d represent the thickness of the excitation beam (0.1 cm), the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette (0.4 cm), and the width of the cuvette (1.0 cm).

The original concentrations of AmB, the absorbance, and fluorescence intensity of Cl, N-CDs with different concentrations of AmB were summarized in Table 3 . The correction factor (CF) of IFE at each concentration of AmB was obtained according to Eq. (2). The observed and corrected fluorescence (after removing IFE) of Cl, N-CDs was figured out and depicted in Fig. 4 a, b. It is found that about 65 % of the total suppressed efficiency stemmed from the IFE of AmB to Cl, N-CDs. The remaining suppressed effect may come from DQE or SQE or both of them. Both DQE and SQE could be theoretically described by the Stern-Volmer equation:
(3)
I 0
/ I =
K SV
·
C AmB
+ 1
Where C AmB indicates the concentration of AmB, I 0 and I are the fluorescence intensity of original Cl, N-CDs, and in the presence of AmB, respectively. As demonstrated in Fig. 4c, d, the ratios of F cor,0/F cor of Cl, N-CDs with/without AmB are linear with the concentrations of AmB, indicating that DQE or SQE should be considered for the fluorescence quenching. The quenching constant of K SV is 0.0734 and 0.0554 μM−1 for fluorescence at 482 and 650 nm, respectively.

The fluorescence lifetime in SQE remains unchanged, while the ratio of the lifetime (τ 0/τ) is proportional to the ratio of I 0/I. Thus, the fluorescence lifetimes of Cl, N-CDs at 482 and 650 nm with the absence and presence of AmB were studied to differentiate DQE and SQE. As demonstrated in Fig. S12, S13, the average fluorescence lifetimes of Cl, N-CDs at 482 and 650 nm were 7.7 and 4.7 ns, which had no obvious changes after detecting AmB (7.7 and 4.1 ns), suggesting that the quenching process involves into a SQE process rather than DQE (Table S3) [29].

Moreover, K SV is further used to clarify SQE or DQE. In DQE,
(4)
K
SV
=
k q
×
τ 0
Where τ 0 is the fluorescence lifetime of Cl, N-CDs without AmB, and k q is the quenching rate constant. In this study, K SV = 0.0734 μM−1 and τ 0 = 7.7 ns for fluorescence at 482 nm, and K SV = 0.0554 μM−1 and τ 0 = 4.7 ns for fluorescence at 650 nm. Correspondingly, the k q was calculated to be 9.51 × 1012 and 1.18 × 1013 M−1s−1, which were much higher than the possible largest value of 1 × 1010 M−1s−1 for a diffusion-controlled process. This result further suggests that the quenching process is not related to the DQE process.

In summary, both the IFE and SQE are responsible for the fluorescence suppression of Cl, N-CDs toward AmB. The abundant hydroxyl groups in AmB molecules may have hydrogen bonding interactions with the surface –COOH, –OH, –NH2 groups of Cl, N-CDs, which may contribute to the high selectivity toward AmB.

Cl, N-codoped CDs with long-wavelength emission and high QY were synthesized by a one-pot solvothermal method using urea, citric acid, and cyanuric chloride as precursors. The Cl, N-CDs were developed as fluorescent sensors for highly selective and sensitive, and rapid response toward AmB. Moreover, the Cl, N-CDs can be practically applied to quantify AmB in milk and wastewater. The paper-based fluorescent sensor with Cl, N-CDs was not available because of the subtle color change, however, this work provides a facile method for the preparation of fluorescent CDs with long emissive wavelength, large stokes shift, and high QY, and develops a new analysis method for determination of AmB in real samples.

Guang-Ming Bao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Shun-Qiang Cui: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Yi-Fan Xia: Visualization, Investigation. Zhen-Chong Dou: Validation, Investigation. Xia Wei: Validation, Investigation. Zhi-Qiang Cai: Visualization, Investigation. Peiyao Chen: Formal analysis. Yi Zhang: Formal analysis. Yu-Xin Chen: Formal analysis. Hou-Qun Yuan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 22165012, 22166018, 31960720) and the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei University of Technology (XJ2022007801).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.111105.
